comorbidities.name: Recent viral upper respiratory tract infection (coryzal prodrome)

pathophysiology_summary: Croup (viral laryngotracheobronchitis), usually caused by parainfluenza virus, produces inflammation and oedema of the subglottic larynx and upper trachea in a young child whose airway is narrowest at the cricoid ring. The subglottic swelling narrows the airway, generating the characteristic seal-like barking cough, hoarse voice and inspiratory stridor, which worsen with crying and agitation (dynamic airway narrowing) and at night. Severity is graded clinically: MILD croup has a barking cough with NO stridor and NO increased work of breathing at rest; stridor and recession present ONLY on agitation indicate mild disease. In this child, stridor and mild recession are present only when crying and resolve completely at rest, he is alert, pink, feeding and swallowing normally with a low-grade fever — a mild presentation. Evidence-based management of croup of any severity is a single dose of oral corticosteroid (dexamethasone or prednisolone), which reduces airway oedema over 2-6 hours; nebulised adrenaline is reserved for moderate-to-severe croup with stridor at rest. The clinical value of assessment is to confirm mild croup, deliver the steroid, and actively exclude the dangerous mimics — acute epiglottitis and bacterial tracheitis — and evolving airway obstruction.

differential.evidence_basis: Sudden seal-like barking cough, hoarse voice and inspiratory stridor after a 2-day coryzal prodrome in a toddler, worse at night, is the classic croup picture.
The most dangerous mimic of noisy breathing in a child — must be actively excluded by drooling, inability to swallow, muffled voice, tripod posture and toxic high fever.
A serious secondary bacterial infection that mimics severe croup; suggested by high fever, systemic toxicity and rapid deterioration — must be held and monitored for.
Can cause acute stridor; distinguished by a sudden choking event in a well child rather than a preceding viral prodrome.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Croup should lead early, but the AI must actively hold epiglottitis and bacterial tracheitis as important-not-to-miss and screen for them, and must not leap to calling 000 for a child who is settled at rest.

differential.evidence_basis: Barking cough, hoarseness and stridor only on agitation with no stridor/recession at rest, alert, pink and feeding — mild croup.
Swallowing saliva, drinking, no drooling, no muffled voice, no tripod posture and only low-grade fever argue strongly against epiglottitis — but it remains a safety-net trigger.
No high-grade toxic fever, alert and non-toxic between coughing bouts, chest clear — bacterial tracheitis unlikely, but deterioration must be safety-netted.
A clear 2-day viral prodrome with no choking event makes an inhaled foreign body very unlikely.

differential_progression.scoring_note: With airway red flags screened negative and the child settled at rest, this is mild croup. The correct action is same-day in-person review to obtain a single dose of corticosteroid and confirm the airway, plus airway safety-netting — NOT 000/ambulance, and NOT pure self-care that misses the steroid.

differential.evidence_basis: Presumptive mild croup; the child needs a single dose of oral corticosteroid today and airway safety-netting. Airway cannot be fully examined remotely, so same-day in-person review is required.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Telehealth cannot auscultate or fully assess the airway, so the position is presumptive mild croup pending same-day in-person review. A confident definitive diagnosis, immediate 000/ambulance for a child stable at rest, or self-care with no steroid are all incorrect.

red_flags.description: Inspiratory stridor (harsh noisy breathing) present when the child is completely calm and at rest, or biphasic/continuous stridor — indicates moderate-to-severe airway narrowing
red_flags.clinical_significance: Stridor at rest marks a move from mild to moderate/severe croup and impending airway compromise, requiring urgent nebulised adrenaline and emergency care. Screened negative — stridor only on agitation here.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progressive subglottic obstruction can lead to respiratory failure and arrest if not escalated urgently.

red_flags.description: Drooling, inability/refusal to swallow, muffled 'hot potato' voice, tripod/forward-leaning posture, toxic high fever — features of acute epiglottitis
red_flags.clinical_significance: Epiglottitis is a life-threatening airway emergency that can occlude the airway rapidly; the child must be kept calm and taken emergently. Screened negative — swallowing saliva, drinking, no drooling, no muffled voice.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed epiglottitis can progress to complete airway obstruction and death within minutes to hours.

red_flags.description: Severe or sustained chest/neck recession, tracheal tug or grunting present at rest, or obvious increased work of breathing when calm
red_flags.clinical_significance: Marked work of breathing at rest indicates significant airway obstruction and fatigue risk, requiring emergency treatment. Screened negative — mild recession only when crying, resolving at rest.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Unrecognised increasing work of breathing can precede exhaustion, hypoxia and respiratory arrest.

red_flags.description: Pallor, cyanosis (blue lips/face), drowsiness, floppiness or difficulty rousing — hypoxia and exhaustion
red_flags.clinical_significance: Cyanosis or altered consciousness signals decompensation and is an immediate life-threat requiring 000. Screened negative — alert, interactive, normal colour.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Cyanosis or lethargy in an obstructed airway is a pre-terminal sign; delay is fatal.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Over-triage of stable mild croup to 000/ambulance
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being alarmed by the frightening noise and calling 000 for a child who is settled, pink and feeding at rest with stridor only on agitation — avoidable emergency transport that itself agitates the child and can worsen the stridor.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Under-triage / missing the steroid and airway net
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Advising simple home comfort measures with no in-person review, missing that even mild croup benefits from a single dose of corticosteroid and requires clear airway safety-netting for overnight deterioration.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Premature closure missing epiglottitis / bacterial tracheitis
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Anchoring on 'just croup' without actively screening for drooling, inability to swallow, toxic fever and stridor at rest that would signal a dangerous mimic or evolving obstruction.
Reaching for a salbutamol inhaler (this is upper-airway oedema, not bronchospasm) or antibiotics (croup is viral) instead of the correct corticosteroid.
Giving a confident definitive diagnosis and reassurance without in-person airway assessment, when the epiglottitis mimic and airway status cannot be fully excluded over chat.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Auscultation of the chest and upper airway (air entry, quality/timing of stridor, wheeze or crepitations)
Clinician-measured vital signs — heart rate, respiratory rate, blood pressure and oxygen saturation (SpO2)
Direct assessment of true work of breathing, tracheal tug and degree of subglottic narrowing
Direct inspection of the oropharynx/airway (deliberately avoided in suspected croup/epiglottitis to prevent provoking spasm) and any confirmatory neck radiograph (steeple sign)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Arrange same-day in-person review (GP, after-hours GP or urgent care / paediatric ED depending on access and time of night) so a clinician can confirm mild croup, assess the airway and give a single dose of oral corticosteroid; keep the child calm and upright, and give explicit airway red-flag safety-netting for immediate 000 escalation. Do not call an ambulance for a child who is settled, pink and feeding at rest, and do not manage at home with no steroid and no review.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The dominant risk is mis-triage in either direction: over-calling 000 for stable mild croup (avoidable, distressing, can worsen stridor), or under-calling with home comfort measures only and missing the steroid, the airway safety-net, or an evolving/severe airway obstruction or epiglottitis.

guidelines_referenced.reference: RCH Melbourne Clinical Practice Guideline: Croup (laryngotracheobronchitis)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Grade severity clinically; MILD croup = barking cough with no stridor or increased work of breathing at rest. Give a single dose of oral corticosteroid (dexamethasone or prednisolone) to all children with croup; nebulised adrenaline is for moderate-to-severe croup with stridor at rest. Keep the child calm and avoid distressing procedures.

Corticosteroid is the mainstay for croup of any severity; antibiotics and bronchodilators are not indicated for uncomplicated viral croup; provide safety-netting for stridor at rest, increasing work of breathing or systemic deterioration.
